steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 1 calls Asimov's flat characters a stylistic weakness. Text 2 pushes back, arguing the minimal characterization is intentional — it serves Asimov's theme that individuals are insignificant across historical epochs. The question asks what the student in Text 2 would say about Text 1's critique.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Asimov's characterizations would have been richer if he had possessed a deeper understanding of history.: Text 2 does not dispute the cause so much as deny it is a flaw at all. This misframes the argument. ✗
  • Choice B — The flatness of Asimov's characters is a feature of *The Caves of Steel* and *The Stars, Like Dust* but not of *Foundation*.: Text 2 makes no such scope limitation. ✗
  • Choice C — The flatness of Asimov's characters should not necessarily be considered a flaw in his writing.: Exactly Text 2's position — the minimal characterization is a deliberate thematic choice, not a weakness. ✓
  • Choice D — Asimov's characterizations would have been more believable if his writing had discussed fewer ideas.: Text 2 does not dispute which characters are flat, only whether flatness is a flaw. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
